    39→class Permission(models.Model):
    40→    """
    41→    The permissions system provides a way to assign permissions to specific
    42→    users and groups of users.
    43→
    44→    The permission system is used by the Django admin site, but may also be
    45→    useful in your own code. The Django admin site uses permissions as follows:
    46→
    47→        - The "add" permission limits the user's ability to view the "add" form
    48→          and add an object.
    49→        - The "change" permission limits a user's ability to view the change
    50→          list, view the "change" form and change an object.
    51→        - The "delete" permission limits the ability to delete an object.
    52→        - The "view" permission limits the ability to view an object.
    53→
    54→    Permissions are set globally per type of object, not per specific object
    55→    instance. It is possible to say "Mary may change news stories," but it's
    56→    not currently possible to say "Mary may change news stories, but only the

   102→class Group(models.Model):

   105→    some other label, to those users. A user can belong to any number of
   106→    groups.
   107→
   108→    A user in a group automatically has all the permissions granted to that
   109→    group. For example, if the group 'Site editors' has the permission
   110→    can_edit_home_page, any user in that group will have that permission.
   111→
   112→    Beyond permissions, groups are a convenient way to categorize users to
   113→    apply some label, or extended functionality, to them. For example, you
   114→    could create a group 'Special users', and you could write code that would
   115→    do special things to those users -- such as giving them access to a
   116→    members-only portion of your site, or sending them members-only email

    51→class BaseIterable:
    52→    def __init__(
    53→        self, queryset, chunked_fetch=False, chunk_size=GET_ITERATOR_CHUNK_SIZE
    54→    ):
    55→        self.queryset = queryset
    56→        self.chunked_fetch = chunked_fetch
    57→        self.chunk_size = chunk_size
    58→
    59→    async def _async_generator(self):
    60→        # Generators don't actually start running until the first time you call
    61→        # next() on them, so make the generator object in the async thread and
    62→        # then repeatedly dispatch to it in a sync thread.
    63→        sync_generator = self.__iter__()
    64→
    65→        def next_slice(gen):
    66→            return list(islice(gen, self.chunk_size))
    67→
    68→        while True:
    69→            chunk = await sync_to_async(next_slice)(sync_generator)
    70→            for item in chunk:
    71→                yield item
    72→            if len(chunk) < self.chunk_size:
    73→                break
    74→
    75→    # __aiter__() is a *synchronous* method that has to then return an
    76→    # *asynchronous* iterator/generator. Thus, nest an async generator inside
    77→    # it.
    78→    # This is a generic iterable converter for now, and is going to suffer a
    79→    # performance penalty on large sets of items due to the cost of crossing
    80→    # over the sync barrier for each chunk. Custom __aiter__() methods should
    81→    # be added to each Iterable subclass, but that needs some work in the
    82→    # Compiler first.
    83→    def __aiter__(self):
    84→        return self._async_generator()

    87→class ModelIterable(BaseIterable):
    88→    """Iterable that yields a model instance for each row."""
    89→
    90→    def __iter__(self):
    91→        queryset = self.queryset
    92→        db = queryset.db
    93→        compiler = queryset.query.get_compiler(using=db)
    94→        fetch_mode = queryset._fetch_mode
    95→        # Execute the query. This will also fill compiler.select, klass_info,
    96→        # and annotations.
    97→        results = compiler.execute_sql(
    98→            chunked_fetch=self.chunked_fetch, chunk_size=self.chunk_size
    99→        )
   100→        select, klass_info, annotation_col_map = (
   101→            compiler.select,
   102→            compiler.klass_info,
   103→            compiler.annotation_col_map,
   104→        )
   105→        model_cls = klass_info["model"]
   106→        select_fields = klass_info["select_fields"]
   107→        model_fields_start, model_fields_end = select_fields[0], select_fields[-1] + 1
   108→        init_list = [
   109→            f[0].target.attname for f in select[model_fields_start:model_fields_end]
   110→        ]
   111→        related_populators = get_related_populators(klass_info, select, db, fetch_mode)
   112→        known_related_objects = [
   113→            (
   114→                field,
   115→                related_objs,
   116→                attnames := [
   117→                    (
   118→                        field.attname
   119→                        if from_field == "self"
   120→                        else queryset.model._meta.get_field(from_field).attname
   121→                    )
   122→                    for from_field in field.from_fields
   123→                ],
   124→                operator.attrgetter(*attnames),
   125→            )
   126→            for field, related_objs in queryset._known_related_objects.items()
   127→        ]
   128→        peers = []
   129→        for row in compiler.results_iter(results):
   130→            obj = model_cls.from_db(
   131→                db,
   132→                init_list,
   133→                row[model_fields_start:model_fields_end],
   134→                fetch_mode=fetch_mode,
   135→            )
   136→            if fetch_mode.track_peers:
   137→                peers.append(weak_ref(obj))
   138→                obj._state.peers = peers
   139→            for rel_populator in related_populators:
   140→                rel_populator.populate(row, obj)
   141→            if annotation_col_map:
   142→                for attr_name, col_pos in annotation_col_map.items():
   143→                    setattr(obj, attr_name, row[col_pos])
   144→
   145→            # Add the known related objects to the model.
   146→            for field, rel_objs, rel_attnames, rel_getter in known_related_objects:
   147→                # Avoid overwriting objects loaded by, e.g., select_related().
   148→                if field.is_cached(obj):
   149→                    continue
   150→                # Avoid fetching potentially deferred attributes that would
   151→                # result in unexpected queries.
   152→                if any(attname not in obj.__dict__ for attname in rel_attnames):
   153→                    continue
   154→                rel_obj_id = rel_getter(obj)
   155→                try:
   156→                    rel_obj = rel_objs[rel_obj_id]
   157→                except KeyError:
   158→                    pass  # May happen in qs1 | qs2 scenarios.
   159→                else:
   160→                    setattr(obj, field.name, rel_obj)
   161→
   162→            yield obj

   165→class RawModelIterable(BaseIterable):
   166→    """
   167→    Iterable that yields a model instance for each row from a raw queryset.
   168→    """
   169→
   170→    def __iter__(self):
   171→        # Cache some things for performance reasons outside the loop.
   172→        db = self.queryset.db
   173→        query = self.queryset.query
   174→        connection = connections[db]
   175→        compiler = connection.ops.compiler("SQLCompiler")(query, connection, db)
   176→        query_iterator = iter(query)
   177→
   178→        try:
   179→            (
   180→                model_init_names,
   181→                model_init_pos,
   182→                annotation_fields,
   183→            ) = self.queryset.resolve_model_init_order()
   184→            model_cls = self.queryset.model
   185→            if any(
   186→                f.attname not in model_init_names for f in model_cls._meta.pk_fields
   187→            ):
   188→                raise exceptions.FieldDoesNotExist(
   189→                    "Raw query must include the primary key"
   190→                )
   191→            fields = [self.queryset.model_fields.get(c) for c in self.queryset.columns]
   192→            cols = [f.get_col(f.model._meta.db_table) if f else None for f in fields]
   193→            converters = compiler.get_converters(cols)
   194→            if converters:
   195→                query_iterator = compiler.apply_converters(query_iterator, converters)
   196→            if compiler.has_composite_fields(cols):
   197→                query_iterator = compiler.composite_fields_to_tuples(
   198→                    query_iterator, cols
   199→                )
   200→            fetch_mode = self.queryset._fetch_mode
   201→            peers = []
   202→            for values in query_iterator:
   203→                # Associate fields to values
   204→                model_init_values = [values[pos] for pos in model_init_pos]
   205→                instance = model_cls.from_db(
   206→                    db, model_init_names, model_init_values, fetch_mode=fetch_mode
   207→                )
   208→                if fetch_mode.track_peers:
   209→                    peers.append(weak_ref(instance))
   210→                    instance._state.peers = peers
   211→                if annotation_fields:
   212→                    for column, pos in annotation_fields:
   213→                        setattr(instance, column, values[pos])
   214→                yield instance
   215→        finally:
   216→            # Done iterating the Query. If it has its own cursor, close it.
   217→            if hasattr(query, "cursor") and query.cursor:
   218→                query.cursor.close()

   221→class ValuesIterable(BaseIterable):
   222→    """
   223→    Iterable returned by QuerySet.values() that yields a dict for each row.
   224→    """
   225→
   226→    def __iter__(self):
   227→        queryset = self.queryset
   228→        query = queryset.query
   229→        compiler = query.get_compiler(queryset.db)
   230→
   231→        if query.selected:
   232→            names = list(query.selected)
   233→        else:
   234→            # extra(select=...) cols are always at the start of the row.
   235→            names = [
   236→                *query.extra_select,
   237→                *query.values_select,
   238→                *query.annotation_select,
   239→            ]
   240→        indexes = range(len(names))
   241→        for row in compiler.results_iter(
   242→            chunked_fetch=self.chunked_fetch, chunk_size=self.chunk_size
   243→        ):
   244→            yield {names[i]: row[i] for i in indexes}

   302→class QuerySet(AltersData):
   303→    """Represent a lazy database lookup for a set of objects."""
   304→
   305→    def __init__(self, model=None, query=None, using=None, hints=None):
   306→        self.model = model
   307→        self._db = using
   308→        self._hints = hints or {}
   309→        self._query = query or sql.Query(self.model)
   310→        self._result_cache = None
   311→        self._sticky_filter = False
   312→        self._for_write = False
   313→        self._prefetch_related_lookups = ()
   314→        self._prefetch_done = False
   315→        self._known_related_objects = {}  # {rel_field: {pk: rel_obj}}
   316→        self._iterable_class = ModelIterable
   317→        self._fetch_mode = FETCH_ONE
   318→        self._fields = None
   319→        self._defer_next_filter = False
   320→        self._deferred_filter = None
   321→
   322→    @property
   323→    def query(self):
   324→        if self._deferred_filter:
   325→            negate, args, kwargs = self._deferred_filter
   326→            self._filter_or_exclude_inplace(negate, args, kwargs)
   327→            self._deferred_filter = None
   328→        return self._query
   329→
   330→    @query.setter
   331→    def query(self, value):
   332→        if value.values_select:
   333→            self._iterable_class = ValuesIterable
   334→        self._query = value

   336→    def as_manager(cls):
   337→        # Address the circular dependency between `Queryset` and `Manager`.
   338→        from django.db.models.manager import Manager
   339→
   340→        manager = Manager.from_queryset(cls)()
   341→        manager._built_with_as_manager = True
   342→        return manager
   343→
   344→    as_manager.queryset_only = True
   345→    as_manager = classmethod(as_manager)

   349→    ########################
   350→
   351→    def __deepcopy__(self, memo):
   352→        """Don't populate the QuerySet's cache."""
   353→        obj = self.__class__()
   354→        for k, v in self.__dict__.items():
   355→            if k == "_result_cache":
   356→                obj.__dict__[k] = None
   357→            else:
   358→                obj.__dict__[k] = copy.deepcopy(v, memo)
   359→        return obj
   360→
   361→    def __getstate__(self):
   362→        # Force the cache to be fully populated.
   363→        self._fetch_all()
   364→        return {**self.__dict__, DJANGO_VERSION_PICKLE_KEY: django.__version__}
   365→
   366→    def __setstate__(self, state):
   367→        pickled_version = state.get(DJANGO_VERSION_PICKLE_KEY)
   368→        if pickled_version:
   369→            if pickled_version != django.__version__:
   370→                warnings.warn(
   371→                    "Pickled queryset instance's Django version %s does not "
   372→                    "match the current version %s."
   373→                    % (pickled_version, django.__version__),
   374→                    RuntimeWarning,
   375→                    stacklevel=2,
   376→                )
   377→        else:
   378→            warnings.warn(
   379→                "Pickled queryset instance's Django version is not specified.",
   380→                RuntimeWarning,
   381→                stacklevel=2,
   382→            )
   383→        self.__dict__.update(state)

   385→    def __repr__(self):
   386→        data = list(self[: REPR_OUTPUT_SIZE + 1])
   387→        if len(data) > REPR_OUTPUT_SIZE:
   388→            data[-1] = "...(remaining elements truncated)..."
   389→        return "<%s %r>" % (self.__class__.__name__, data)
   390→
   391→    def __len__(self):
   392→        self._fetch_all()
   393→        return len(self._result_cache)
   394→
   395→    def __iter__(self):
   396→        """
   397→        The queryset iterator protocol uses three nested iterators in the
   398→        default case:
   399→            1. sql.compiler.execute_sql()
   400→               - Returns 100 rows at time (constants.GET_ITERATOR_CHUNK_SIZE)
   401→                 using cursor.fetchmany(). This part is responsible for
   402→                 doing some column masking, and returning the rows in chunks.
   403→            2. sql.compiler.results_iter()
   404→               - Returns one row at time. At this point the rows are still just
   405→                 tuples. In some cases the return values are converted to
   406→                 Python values at this location.
   407→            3. self.iterator()
   408→               - Responsible for turning the rows into model objects.
   409→        """
   410→        self._fetch_all()
   411→        return iter(self._result_cache)
   412→
   413→    def __aiter__(self):
   414→        # Remember, __aiter__ itself is synchronous, it's the thing it returns
   415→        # that is async!
   416→        async def generator():
   417→            await sync_to_async(self._fetch_all)()
   418→            for item in self._result_cache:
   419→                yield item
   420→
   421→        return generator()
   422→
   423→    def __bool__(self):
   424→        self._fetch_all()
   425→        return bool(self._result_cache)

   427→    def __getitem__(self, k):
   428→        """Retrieve an item or slice from the set of results."""
   429→        if not isinstance(k, (int, slice)):
   430→            raise TypeError(
   431→                "QuerySet indices must be integers or slices, not %s."
   432→                % type(k).__name__
   433→            )
   434→        if (isinstance(k, int) and k < 0) or (
   435→            isinstance(k, slice)
   436→            and (
   437→                (k.start is not None and k.start < 0)
   438→                or (k.stop is not None and k.stop < 0)
   439→            )
   440→        ):
   441→            raise ValueError("Negative indexing is not supported.")
   442→
   443→        if self._result_cache is not None:
   444→            return self._result_cache[k]
   445→
   446→        if isinstance(k, slice):
   447→            qs = self._chain()
   448→            if k.start is not None:
   449→                start = int(k.start)
   450→            else:
   451→                start = None
   452→            if k.stop is not None:
   453→                stop = int(k.stop)
   454→            else:
   455→                stop = None
   456→            qs.query.set_limits(start, stop)
   457→            return list(qs)[:: k.step] if k.step else qs
   458→
   459→        qs = self._chain()
   460→        qs.query.set_limits(k, k + 1)
   461→        qs._fetch_all()
   462→        return qs._result_cache[0]

   465→        return cls

   467→    def __and__(self, other):
   468→        self._check_operator_queryset(other, "&")
   469→        self._merge_sanity_check(other)
   470→        if isinstance(other, EmptyQuerySet):
   471→            return other
   472→        if isinstance(self, EmptyQuerySet):
   473→            return self
   474→        combined = self._chain()
   475→        combined._merge_known_related_objects(other)
   476→        combined.query.combine(other.query, sql.AND)
   477→        return combined
   478→
   479→    def __or__(self, other):
   480→        self._check_operator_queryset(other, "|")
   481→        self._merge_sanity_check(other)
   482→        if isinstance(self, EmptyQuerySet):
   483→            return other
   484→        if isinstance(other, EmptyQuerySet):
   485→            return self
   486→        query = (
   487→            self
   488→            if self.query.can_filter()
   489→            else self.model._base_manager.filter(pk__in=self.values("pk"))
   490→        )
   491→        combined = query._chain()
   492→        combined._merge_known_related_objects(other)
   493→        if not other.query.can_filter():
   494→            other = other.model._base_manager.filter(pk__in=other.values("pk"))
   495→        combined.query.combine(other.query, sql.OR)
   496→        return combined
   497→
   498→    def __xor__(self, other):
   499→        self._check_operator_queryset(other, "^")
   500→        self._merge_sanity_check(other)
   501→        if isinstance(self, EmptyQuerySet):
   502→            return other
   503→        if isinstance(other, EmptyQuerySet):
   504→            return self
   505→        query = (
   506→            self
   507→            if self.query.can_filter()
   508→            else self.model._base_manager.filter(pk__in=self.values("pk"))
   509→        )
   510→        combined = query._chain()
   511→        combined._merge_known_related_objects(other)
   512→        if not other.query.can_filter():
   513→            other = other.model._base_manager.filter(pk__in=other.values("pk"))
   514→        combined.query.combine(other.query, sql.XOR)
   515→        return combined

   521→    def _iterator(self, use_chunked_fetch, chunk_size):
   522→        iterable = self._iterable_class(
   523→            self,
   524→            chunked_fetch=use_chunked_fetch,
   525→            chunk_size=chunk_size or 2000,
   526→        )
   527→        if not self._prefetch_related_lookups or chunk_size is None:
   528→            yield from iterable
   529→            return
   530→
   531→        iterator = iter(iterable)
   532→        while results := list(islice(iterator, chunk_size)):
   533→            prefetch_related_objects(results, *self._prefetch_related_lookups)
   534→            yield from results
   535→
   536→    def iterator(self, chunk_size=None):
   537→        """
   538→        An iterator over the results from applying this QuerySet to the
   539→        database. chunk_size must be provided for QuerySets that prefetch
   540→        related objects. Otherwise, a default chunk_size of 2000 is supplied.
   541→        """
   542→        if chunk_size is None:
   543→            if self._prefetch_related_lookups:
   544→                raise ValueError(
   545→                    "chunk_size must be provided when using QuerySet.iterator() after "
   546→                    "prefetch_related()."
   547→                )

   875→    def _handle_order_with_respect_to(self, objs):

   877→            get_filter_kwargs_for_object = order_wrt.get_filter_kwargs_for_object
   878→            attnames = list(get_filter_kwargs_for_object(objs[0]))
   879→            group_keys = set()
   880→            obj_groups = []
   881→            for obj in objs:
   882→                group_key = tuple(get_filter_kwargs_for_object(obj).values())
   883→                group_keys.add(group_key)
   884→                obj_groups.append((obj, group_key))
   885→            filters = [
   886→                Q.create(list(zip(attnames, group_key))) for group_key in group_keys
   887→            ]
   888→            next_orders = (
   889→                self.model._base_manager.using(self.db)
   890→                .filter(reduce(operator.or_, filters))
   891→                .values_list(*attnames)
   892→                .annotate(_order__max=Max("_order") + 1)
   893→            )
   894→            # Create mapping of group values to max order.
   895→            group_next_orders = dict.fromkeys(group_keys, 0)
   896→            group_next_orders.update(
   897→                (tuple(group_key), next_order) for *group_key, next_order in next_orders
   898→            )
   899→            # Assign _order values to new objects.
   900→            for obj, group_key in obj_groups:
   901→                if getattr(obj, "_order", None) is None:
   902→                    group_next_order = group_next_orders[group_key]
   903→                    obj._order = group_next_order
   904→                    group_next_orders[group_key] += 1

   928→    def bulk_update(self, objs, fields, batch_size=None):
   929→        """
   930→        Update the given fields in each of the given objects in the database.
   931→        """
   932→        if batch_size is not None and batch_size <= 0:
   933→            raise ValueError("Batch size must be a positive integer.")
   934→        if not fields:
   935→            raise ValueError("Field names must be given to bulk_update().")
   936→        objs = tuple(objs)
   937→        if not all(obj._is_pk_set() for obj in objs):
   938→            raise ValueError("All bulk_update() objects must have a primary key set.")

   940→        fields = [opts.get_field(name) for name in fields]

   942→            raise ValueError("bulk_update() can only be used with concrete fields.")

   944→        for parent in opts.all_parents:
   945→            all_pk_fields.update(parent._meta.pk_fields)
   946→        if any(f in all_pk_fields for f in fields):
   947→            raise ValueError("bulk_update() cannot be used with primary key fields.")
   948→        if not objs:
   949→            return 0
   950→        for obj in objs:
   951→            obj._prepare_related_fields_for_save(
   952→                operation_name="bulk_update", fields=fields
   953→            )
   954→        # PK is used twice in the resulting update query, once in the filter
   955→        # and once in the WHEN. Each field will also have one CAST.
   956→        self._for_write = True
   957→        connection = connections[self.db]
   958→        max_batch_size = connection.ops.bulk_batch_size(
   959→            [opts.pk, opts.pk, *fields], objs

   961→        batch_size = min(batch_size, max_batch_size) if batch_size else max_batch_size
   962→        requires_casting = connection.features.requires_casted_case_in_updates
   963→        batches = (objs[i : i + batch_size] for i in range(0, len(objs), batch_size))
   964→        updates = []
   965→        for batch_objs in batches:
   966→            update_kwargs = {}
   967→            for field in fields:
   968→                when_statements = []
   969→                for obj in batch_objs:
   970→                    attr = getattr(obj, field.attname)
   971→                    if not hasattr(attr, "resolve_expression"):
   972→                        attr = Value(attr, output_field=field)
   973→                    when_statements.append(When(pk=obj.pk, then=attr))
   974→                case_statement = Case(*when_statements, output_field=field)
   975→                if requires_casting:
   976→                    case_statement = Cast(case_statement, output_field=field)
   977→                update_kwargs[field.attname] = case_statement
   978→            updates.append(([obj.pk for obj in batch_objs], update_kwargs))

   980→        queryset = self.using(self.db)
   981→        with transaction.atomic(using=self.db, savepoint=False):
   982→            for pks, update_kwargs in updates:
   983→                rows_updated += queryset.filter(pk__in=pks).update(**update_kwargs)
   984→        return rows_updated

    25→    get_context_data() as the template context.

    37→class View:

    39→    Intentionally simple parent class for all views. Only implements
    40→    dispatch-by-method and simple sanity checking.
    41→    """

    44→        "get",
    45→        "post",
    46→        "put",
    47→        "patch",
    48→        "delete",
    49→        "head",
    50→        "options",
    51→        "trace",

    54→    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
    55→        """
    56→        Constructor. Called in the URLconf; can contain helpful extra
    57→        keyword arguments, and other things.
    58→        """
    59→        # Go through keyword arguments, and either save their values to our
    60→        # instance, or raise an error.
    61→        for key, value in kwargs.items():
    62→            setattr(self, key, value)

    64→    @classproperty
    65→    def view_is_async(cls):

    67→            getattr(cls, method)
    68→            for method in cls.http_method_names

    70→        ]
    71→        if not handlers:
    72→            return False
    73→        is_async = iscoroutinefunction(handlers[0])
    74→        if not all(iscoroutinefunction(h) == is_async for h in handlers[1:]):
    75→            raise ImproperlyConfigured(
    76→                f"{cls.__qualname__} HTTP handlers must either be all sync or all "
    77→                "async."
    78→            )
    79→        return is_async

    81→    @classonlymethod
    82→    def as_view(cls, **initkwargs):

    84→        for key in initkwargs:

    86→                raise TypeError(
    87→                    "The method name %s is not accepted as a keyword argument "
    88→                    "to %s()." % (key, cls.__name__)
    89→                )
    90→            if not hasattr(cls, key):
    91→                raise TypeError(
    92→                    "%s() received an invalid keyword %r. as_view "
    93→                    "only accepts arguments that are already "
    94→                    "attributes of the class." % (cls.__name__, key)
    95→                )
    96→
    97→        def view(request, *args, **kwargs):
    98→            self = cls(**initkwargs)
    99→            self.setup(request, *args, **kwargs)

   101→                raise AttributeError(
   102→                    "%s instance has no 'request' attribute. Did you override "
   103→                    "setup() and forget to call super()?" % cls.__name__

   111→        # view_class should be used to robustly determine the name of the view
   112→        # instead.
   113→        view.__doc__ = cls.__doc__
   114→        view.__module__ = cls.__module__

   117→        # the dispatch method.

   129→            self.head = self.get

   131→        self.args = args
   132→        self.kwargs = kwargs

   134→    def dispatch(self, request, *args, **kwargs):

   136→        # defer to the error handler. Also defer to the error handler if the
   137→        # request method isn't on the approved list.
   138→        method = request.method.lower()

   140→            handler = getattr(self, method, self.http_method_not_allowed)
   141→        else:
   142→            handler = self.http_method_not_allowed

   145→    def http_method_not_allowed(self, request, *args, **kwargs):

   149→            request.method,
   150→            request.path,
   151→            response=response,
   152→            request=request,
   153→        )
   154→
   155→        if self.view_is_async:
   156→
   157→            async def func():

   160→            return func()
   161→        else:

   164→    def options(self, request, *args, **kwargs):

   166→        response = HttpResponse()

   170→        if self.view_is_async:
   171→
   172→            async def func():
   173→                return response
   174→
   175→            return func()
   176→        else:
   177→            return response
   178→
   179→    def _allowed_methods(self):
   180→        return [m.upper() for m in self.http_method_names if hasattr(self, m)]

   183→class TemplateResponseMixin:
   184→    """A mixin that can be used to render a template."""
   185→
   186→    template_name = None
   187→    template_engine = None
   188→    response_class = TemplateResponse
   189→    content_type = None
   190→
   191→    def render_to_response(self, context, **response_kwargs):
   192→        """
   193→        Return a response, using the `response_class` for this view, with a
   194→        template rendered with the given context.
   195→
   196→        Pass response_kwargs to the constructor of the response class.
   197→        """
   198→        response_kwargs.setdefault("content_type", self.content_type)
   199→        return self.response_class(
   200→            request=self.request,
   201→            template=self.get_template_names(),
   202→            context=context,
   203→            using=self.template_engine,
   204→            **response_kwargs,
   205→        )
   206→
   207→    def get_template_names(self):
   208→        """
   209→        Return a list of template names to be used for the request. Must return
   210→        a list. May not be called if render_to_response() is overridden.
   211→        """
   212→        if self.template_name is None:
   213→            raise ImproperlyConfigured(
   214→                "TemplateResponseMixin requires either a definition of "
   215→                "'template_name' or an implementation of 'get_template_names()'"
   216→            )
   217→        else:
   218→            return [self.template_name]
   219→

   221→class TemplateView(TemplateResponseMixin, ContextMixin, View):

   224→    """
   225→
   226→    def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
   227→        context = self.get_context_data(**kwargs)
   228→        return self.render_to_response(context)

   231→class RedirectView(View):
   232→    """Provide a redirect on any GET request."""
   233→
   234→    permanent = False
   235→    url = None
   236→    pattern_name = None
   237→    query_string = False
   238→
   239→    def get_redirect_url(self, *args, **kwargs):
   240→        """
   241→        Return the URL redirect to. Keyword arguments from the URL pattern
   242→        match generating the redirect request are provided as kwargs to this
   243→        method.
   244→        """
   245→        if self.url:
   246→            url = self.url % kwargs
   247→        elif self.pattern_name:
   248→            url = reverse(self.pattern_name, args=args, kwargs=kwargs)
   249→        else:
   250→            return None
   251→
   252→        args = self.request.META.get("QUERY_STRING", "")
   253→        if args and self.query_string:
   254→            if urlparse(url).query:
   255→                url = f"{url}&{args}"
   256→            else:
   257→                url = f"{url}?{args}"
   258→        return url
   259→
   260→    def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
   261→        url = self.get_redirect_url(*args, **kwargs)
   262→        if url:
   263→            if self.permanent:
   264→                return HttpResponsePermanentRedirect(url)
   265→            else:
   266→                return HttpResponseRedirect(url)
   267→        else:
   268→            response = HttpResponseGone()
   269→            log_response("Gone: %s", request.path, response=response, request=request)
   270→            return response

  1357→                alias = alias.removeprefix("-")
  1358→                descending = True

  1510→                warnings.warn(
  1511→                    "Calling values_list() with no field name and flat=True "
  1512→                    "is deprecated. Pass an explicit field name instead, like "
  1513→                    "'pk'.",
  1514→                    RemovedInDjango70Warning,

  1517→
  1518→        field_names = {f: False for f in fields if not hasattr(f, "resolve_expression")}
  1519→        _fields = []
  1520→        expressions = {}
  1521→        counter = 1
  1522→        for field in fields:
  1523→            field_name = field
  1524→            expression = None
  1525→            if hasattr(field, "resolve_expression"):
  1526→                field_name = getattr(
  1527→                    field, "default_alias", field.__class__.__name__.lower()

  1534→                seen = True

  1956→    @property
  1957→    def ordered(self):
  1958→        """
  1959→        Return True if the QuerySet is ordered -- i.e. has an order_by()
  1960→        clause or a default ordering on the model (or is empty).
  1961→        """

  1964→        if self.query.extra_order_by or self.query.order_by:
  1965→            return True
  1966→        elif (
  1967→            self.query.default_ordering
  1968→            and self.query.get_meta().ordering
  1969→            and
  1970→            # A default ordering doesn't affect GROUP BY queries.
  1971→            not self.query.group_by
  1972→        ):

  1977→    @property
  1978→    def totally_ordered(self):
  1979→        """
  1980→        Returns True if the QuerySet is ordered and the ordering is
  1981→        deterministic. This requires that the ordering includes a field
  1982→        (or set of fields) that is unique and non-nullable.
  1983→
  1984→        For queries involving a GROUP BY clause, the model's default
  1985→        ordering is ignored. Ordering specified via .extra(order_by=...)
  1986→        is also ignored.
  1987→        """
  1988→        if not self.ordered:
  1989→            return False
  1990→        ordering = self.query.order_by
  1991→        if not ordering and self.query.default_ordering:
  1992→            ordering = self.query.get_meta().ordering
  1993→        if not ordering:
  1994→            return False
  1995→        opts = self.model._meta
  1996→        pk_fields = {f.attname for f in opts.pk_fields}
  1997→        ordering_fields = set()
  1998→        for part in ordering:
  1999→            # Search for single field providing a total ordering.

2000→            field_name = None
  2001→            if isinstance(part, str):
  2002→                field_name = part.lstrip("-")
  2003→            elif isinstance(part, F):
  2004→                field_name = part.name
  2005→            elif isinstance(part, OrderBy) and isinstance(part.expression, F):
  2006→                field_name = part.expression.name
  2007→            if field_name:
  2008→                if field_name == "pk":
  2009→                    return True
  2010→                # Normalize attname references by using get_field().
  2011→                try:
  2012→                    field = opts.get_field(field_name)
  2013→                except exceptions.FieldDoesNotExist:
  2014→                    # Could be "?" for random ordering or a related field
  2015→                    # lookup. Skip this part of introspection for now.
  2016→                    continue
  2017→                # Ordering by a related field name orders by the referenced
  2018→                # model's ordering. Skip this part of introspection for now.
  2019→                if field.remote_field and field_name == field.name:
  2020→                    continue
  2021→                if field.attname in pk_fields and len(pk_fields) == 1:
  2022→                    return True
  2023→                if field.unique and not field.null:
  2024→                    return True
  2025→                ordering_fields.add(field.attname)
  2026→
  2027→        # Account for members of a CompositePrimaryKey.
  2028→        if ordering_fields.issuperset(pk_fields):
  2029→            return True
  2030→        # No single total ordering field, try unique_together and total
  2031→        # unique constraints.
  2032→        constraint_field_names = (
  2033→            *opts.unique_together,
  2034→            *(constraint.fields for constraint in opts.total_unique_constraints),
  2035→        )
  2036→        for field_names in constraint_field_names:
  2037→            # Normalize attname references by using get_field().
  2038→            try:
  2039→                fields = [opts.get_field(field_name) for field_name in field_names]
  2040→            except exceptions.FieldDoesNotExist:
  2041→                continue
  2042→            # Composite unique constraints containing a nullable column
  2043→            # cannot ensure total ordering.
  2044→            if any(field.null for field in fields):
  2045→                continue
  2046→            if ordering_fields.issuperset(field.attname for field in fields):
  2047→                return True
  2048→
  2049→        return False

  2092→    def _batched_insert(

  2127→                        fields=fields,
  2128→                        using=self.db,
  2129→                        on_conflict=on_conflict,
  2130→                        update_fields=update_fields,
  2131→                        unique_fields=unique_fields,
  2132→                        returning_fields=returning_fields,
  2133→                    )
  2134→                )

  2314→    def resolve_model_init_order(self):

  2316→        converter = connections[self.db].introspection.identifier_converter

  2318→            field
  2319→            for column_name, field in self.model_fields.items()
  2320→            if column_name in self.columns

  2323→            (column, pos)
  2324→            for pos, column in enumerate(self.columns)
  2325→            if column not in self.model_fields
  2326→        ]
  2327→        model_init_order = [
  2328→            self.columns.index(converter(f.column)) for f in model_init_fields
  2329→        ]

  2331→        return model_init_names, model_init_order, annotation_fields

  2346→    def _clone(self):

  2406→            model=self.model,

  2414→    @cached_property

  2422→        for query_name, model_name in self.translations.items():

  2425→                index = columns.index(query_name)

  2445→class Prefetch:
  2446→    def __init__(self, lookup, queryset=None, to_attr=None):
  2447→        # `prefetch_through` is the path we traverse to perform the prefetch.
  2448→        self.prefetch_through = lookup
  2449→        # `prefetch_to` is the path to the attribute that stores the result.
  2450→        self.prefetch_to = lookup
  2451→        if queryset is not None and (
  2452→            isinstance(queryset, RawQuerySet)
  2453→            or (
  2454→                hasattr(queryset, "_iterable_class")
  2455→                and not issubclass(queryset._iterable_class, ModelIterable)
  2456→            )
  2457→        ):
  2458→            raise ValueError(
  2459→                "Prefetch querysets cannot use raw(), values(), and values_list()."
  2460→            )
  2461→        if to_attr:
  2462→            self.prefetch_to = LOOKUP_SEP.join(
  2463→                lookup.split(LOOKUP_SEP)[:-1] + [to_attr]
  2464→            )
  2465→
  2466→        self.queryset = queryset
  2467→        self.to_attr = to_attr
  2468→
  2469→    def __getstate__(self):
  2470→        obj_dict = self.__dict__.copy()
  2471→        if self.queryset is not None:
  2472→            queryset = self.queryset._chain()
  2473→            # Prevent the QuerySet from being evaluated
  2474→            queryset._result_cache = []
  2475→            queryset._prefetch_done = True
  2476→            obj_dict["queryset"] = queryset
  2477→        return obj_dict
  2478→
  2479→    def add_prefix(self, prefix):
  2480→        self.prefetch_through = prefix + LOOKUP_SEP + self.prefetch_through
  2481→        self.prefetch_to = prefix + LOOKUP_SEP + self.prefetch_to
  2482→
  2483→    def get_current_prefetch_to(self, level):
  2484→        return LOOKUP_SEP.join(self.prefetch_to.split(LOOKUP_SEP)[: level + 1])
  2485→
  2486→    def get_current_to_attr(self, level):
  2487→        parts = self.prefetch_to.split(LOOKUP_SEP)
  2488→        to_attr = parts[level]
  2489→        as_attr = self.to_attr and level == len(parts) - 1
  2490→        return to_attr, as_attr
  2491→
  2492→    def get_current_querysets(self, level):
  2493→        if (
  2494→            self.get_current_prefetch_to(level) == self.prefetch_to
  2495→            and self.queryset is not None
  2496→        ):
  2497→            return [self.queryset]
  2498→        return None
  2499→
  2500→    def __eq__(self, other):
  2501→        if not isinstance(other, Prefetch):
  2502→            return NotImplemented
  2503→        return self.prefetch_to == other.prefetch_to
  2504→
  2505→    def __hash__(self):
  2506→        return hash((self.__class__, self.prefetch_to))
  2507→

  2509→def normalize_prefetch_lookups(lookups, prefix=None):
  2510→    """Normalize lookups into Prefetch objects."""
  2511→    ret = []
  2512→    for lookup in lookups:
  2513→        if not isinstance(lookup, Prefetch):
  2514→            lookup = Prefetch(lookup)
  2515→        if prefix:
  2516→            lookup.add_prefix(prefix)
  2517→        ret.append(lookup)
  2518→    return ret
  2519→

  2521→def prefetch_related_objects(model_instances, *related_lookups):
  2522→    """
  2523→    Populate prefetched object caches for an iterable of model instances based
  2524→    on the lookups/Prefetch instances given.
  2525→    """
  2526→    if not model_instances:
  2527→        return  # nothing to do
  2528→
  2529→    # We need to be able to dynamically add to the list of prefetch_related
  2530→    # lookups that we look up (see below). So we need some book keeping to
  2531→    # ensure we don't do duplicate work.
  2532→    done_queries = {}  # dictionary of things like 'foo__bar': [results]
  2533→
  2534→    auto_lookups = set()  # we add to this as we go through.
  2535→    followed_descriptors = set()  # recursion protection
  2536→
  2537→    all_lookups = normalize_prefetch_lookups(reversed(related_lookups))
  2538→    while all_lookups:
  2539→        lookup = all_lookups.pop()
  2540→        if lookup.prefetch_to in done_queries:
  2541→            if lookup.queryset is not None:
  2542→                raise ValueError(
  2543→                    "'%s' lookup was already seen with a different queryset. "
  2544→                    "You may need to adjust the ordering of your lookups."
  2545→                    % lookup.prefetch_to
  2546→                )
  2547→
  2548→            continue
  2549→
  2550→        # Top level, the list of objects to decorate is the result cache
  2551→        # from the primary QuerySet. It won't be for deeper levels.
  2552→        obj_list = model_instances
  2553→
  2554→        through_attrs = lookup.prefetch_through.split(LOOKUP_SEP)
  2555→        for level, through_attr in enumerate(through_attrs):
  2556→            # Prepare main instances
  2557→            if not obj_list:
  2558→                break
  2559→
  2560→            prefetch_to = lookup.get_current_prefetch_to(level)
  2561→            if prefetch_to in done_queries:
  2562→                # Skip any prefetching, and any object preparation
  2563→                obj_list = done_queries[prefetch_to]
  2564→                continue
  2565→
  2566→            # Prepare objects:
  2567→            good_objects = True
  2568→            for obj in obj_list:
  2569→                # Since prefetching can re-use instances, it is possible to
  2570→                # have the same instance multiple times in obj_list, so obj
  2571→                # might already be prepared.
  2572→                if not hasattr(obj, "_prefetched_objects_cache"):
  2573→                    try:
  2574→                        obj._prefetched_objects_cache = {}
  2575→                    except (AttributeError, TypeError):
  2576→                        # Must be an immutable object from
  2577→                        # values_list(flat=True), for example (TypeError) or
  2578→                        # a QuerySet subclass that isn't returning Model
  2579→                        # instances (AttributeError), either in Django or a 3rd
  2580→                        # party. prefetch_related() doesn't make sense, so
  2581→                        # quit.
  2582→                        good_objects = False
  2583→                        break
  2584→            if not good_objects:
  2585→                break
  2586→
  2587→            # Descend down tree
  2588→
  2589→            # We assume that objects retrieved are homogeneous (which is the
  2590→            # premise of prefetch_related), so what applies to first object
  2591→            # applies to all.
  2592→            first_obj = next(iter(obj_list))
  2593→            to_attr = lookup.get_current_to_attr(level)[0]
  2594→            prefetcher, descriptor, attr_found, is_fetched = get_prefetcher(
  2595→                first_obj, through_attr, to_attr
  2596→            )
  2597→
  2598→            if not attr_found:
  2599→                raise AttributeError(
  2600→                    "Cannot find '%s' on %s object, '%s' is an invalid "
  2601→                    "parameter to prefetch_related()"
  2602→                    % (
  2603→                        through_attr,
  2604→                        first_obj.__class__.__name__,
  2605→                        lookup.prefetch_through,
  2606→                    )
  2607→                )
  2608→
  2609→            if level == len(through_attrs) - 1 and prefetcher is None:
  2610→                # Last one, this *must* resolve to something that supports
  2611→                # prefetching, otherwise there is no point adding it and the
  2612→                # developer asking for it has made a mistake.
  2613→                raise ValueError(
  2614→                    "'%s' does not resolve to an item that supports "
  2615→                    "prefetching - this is an invalid parameter to "
  2616→                    "prefetch_related()." % lookup.prefetch_through
  2617→                )
  2618→
  2619→            obj_to_fetch = None
  2620→            if prefetcher is not None:
  2621→                obj_to_fetch = [obj for obj in obj_list if not is_fetched(obj)]
  2622→
  2623→            if obj_to_fetch:
  2624→                obj_list, additional_lookups = prefetch_one_level(
  2625→                    obj_to_fetch,
  2626→                    prefetcher,
  2627→                    lookup,
  2628→                    level,
  2629→                )
  2630→                # We need to ensure we don't keep adding lookups from the
  2631→                # same relationships to stop infinite recursion. So, if we
  2632→                # are already on an automatically added lookup, don't add
  2633→                # the new lookups from relationships we've seen already.
  2634→                if not (
  2635→                    prefetch_to in done_queries
  2636→                    and lookup in auto_lookups
  2637→                    and descriptor in followed_descriptors
  2638→                ):
  2639→                    done_queries[prefetch_to] = obj_list
  2640→                    new_lookups = normalize_prefetch_lookups(
  2641→                        reversed(additional_lookups), prefetch_to
  2642→                    )
  2643→                    auto_lookups.update(new_lookups)
  2644→                    all_lookups.extend(new_lookups)
  2645→                followed_descriptors.add(descriptor)
  2646→            else:
  2647→                # Either a singly related object that has already been fetched
  2648→                # (e.g. via select_related), or hopefully some other property
  2649→                # that doesn't support prefetching but needs to be traversed.
  2650→
  2651→                # We replace the current list of parent objects with the list
  2652→                # of related objects, filtering out empty or missing values so
  2653→                # that we can continue with nullable or reverse relations.
  2654→                new_obj_list = []
  2655→                for obj in obj_list:
  2656→                    if through_attr in getattr(obj, "_prefetched_objects_cache", ()):
  2657→                        # If related objects have been prefetched, use the
  2658→                        # cache rather than the object's through_attr.
  2659→                        new_obj = list(obj._prefetched_objects_cache.get(through_attr))
  2660→                    else:
  2661→                        try:
  2662→                            new_obj = getattr(obj, through_attr)
  2663→                        except exceptions.ObjectDoesNotExist:
  2664→                            continue
  2665→                    if new_obj is None:
  2666→                        continue
  2667→                    # We special-case `list` rather than something more generic
  2668→                    # like `Iterable` because we don't want to accidentally
  2669→                    # match user models that define __iter__.
  2670→                    if isinstance(new_obj, list):
  2671→                        new_obj_list.extend(new_obj)
  2672→                    else:
  2673→                        new_obj_list.append(new_obj)
  2674→                obj_list = new_obj_list
  2675→
  2676→
  2677→async def aprefetch_related_objects(model_instances, *related_lookups):
  2678→    """See prefetch_related_objects()."""
  2679→    return await sync_to_async(prefetch_related_objects)(
  2680→        model_instances, *related_lookups
  2681→    )

  2684→def get_prefetcher(instance, through_attr, to_attr):
  2685→    """
  2686→    For the attribute 'through_attr' on the given instance, find
  2687→    an object that has a get_prefetch_querysets().
  2688→    Return a 4 tuple containing:
  2689→    (the object with get_prefetch_querysets (or None),
  2690→     the descriptor object representing this relationship (or None),
  2691→     a boolean that is False if the attribute was not found at all,
  2692→     a function that takes an instance and returns a boolean that is True if
  2693→     the attribute has already been fetched for that instance)
  2694→    """
  2695→
  2696→    def is_to_attr_fetched(model, to_attr):
  2697→        # Special case cached_property instances because hasattr() triggers
  2698→        # attribute computation and assignment.
  2699→        if isinstance(getattr(model, to_attr, None), cached_property):
  2700→
  2701→            def has_cached_property(instance):
  2702→                return to_attr in instance.__dict__
  2703→
  2704→            return has_cached_property
  2705→
  2706→        def has_to_attr_attribute(instance):
  2707→            return hasattr(instance, to_attr)
  2708→
  2709→        return has_to_attr_attribute
  2710→
  2711→    prefetcher = None
  2712→    is_fetched = is_to_attr_fetched(instance.__class__, to_attr)
  2713→
  2714→    # For singly related objects, we have to avoid getting the attribute
  2715→    # from the object, as this will trigger the query. So we first try
  2716→    # on the class, in order to get the descriptor object.
  2717→    rel_obj_descriptor = getattr(instance.__class__, through_attr, None)
  2718→    if rel_obj_descriptor is None:
  2719→        attr_found = hasattr(instance, through_attr)
  2720→    else:
  2721→        attr_found = True
  2722→        if rel_obj_descriptor:
  2723→            # singly related object, descriptor object has the
  2724→            # get_prefetch_querysets() method.
  2725→            if hasattr(rel_obj_descriptor, "get_prefetch_querysets"):
  2726→                prefetcher = rel_obj_descriptor
  2727→                # If to_attr is set, check if the value has already been set,
  2728→                # which is done with has_to_attr_attribute(). Do not use the
  2729→                # method from the descriptor, as the cache_name it defines
  2730→                # checks the field name, not the to_attr value.
  2731→                if through_attr == to_attr:
  2732→                    is_fetched = rel_obj_descriptor.is_cached
  2733→            else:
  2734→                # descriptor doesn't support prefetching, so we go ahead and
  2735→                # get the attribute on the instance rather than the class to
  2736→                # support many related managers
  2737→                rel_obj = getattr(instance, through_attr)
  2738→                if hasattr(rel_obj, "get_prefetch_querysets"):
  2739→                    prefetcher = rel_obj
  2740→                if through_attr == to_attr:
  2741→
  2742→                    def in_prefetched_cache(instance):
  2743→                        return through_attr in instance._prefetched_objects_cache
  2744→
  2745→                    is_fetched = in_prefetched_cache
  2746→    return prefetcher, rel_obj_descriptor, attr_found, is_fetched
  2747→

  2749→def prefetch_one_level(instances, prefetcher, lookup, level):
  2750→    """
  2751→    Helper function for prefetch_related_objects().
  2752→
  2753→    Run prefetches on all instances using the prefetcher object,
  2754→    assigning results to relevant caches in instance.
  2755→
  2756→    Return the prefetched objects along with any additional prefetches that
  2757→    must be done due to prefetch_related lookups found from default managers.
  2758→    """
  2759→    # prefetcher must have a method get_prefetch_querysets() which takes a list
  2760→    # of instances, and returns a tuple:
  2761→
  2762→    # (queryset of instances of self.model that are related to passed in
  2763→    #  instances,
  2764→    #  callable that gets value to be matched for returned instances,
  2765→    #  callable that gets value to be matched for passed in instances,
  2766→    #  boolean that is True for singly related objects,
  2767→    #  cache or field name to assign to,
  2768→    #  boolean that is True when the previous argument is a cache name vs a
  2769→    #  field name).
  2770→
  2771→    # The 'values to be matched' must be hashable as they will be used
  2772→    # in a dictionary.
  2773→    (
  2774→        rel_qs,
  2775→        rel_obj_attr,
  2776→        instance_attr,
  2777→        single,
  2778→        cache_name,
  2779→        is_descriptor,
  2780→    ) = prefetcher.get_prefetch_querysets(
  2781→        instances, lookup.get_current_querysets(level)
  2782→    )
  2783→    # We have to handle the possibility that the QuerySet we just got back
  2784→    # contains some prefetch_related lookups. We don't want to trigger the
  2785→    # prefetch_related functionality by evaluating the query. Rather, we need
  2786→    # to merge in the prefetch_related lookups.
  2787→    # Copy the lookups in case it is a Prefetch object which could be reused
  2788→    # later (happens in nested prefetch_related).
  2789→    additional_lookups = [
  2790→        copy.copy(additional_lookup)
  2791→        for additional_lookup in getattr(rel_qs, "_prefetch_related_lookups", ())
  2792→    ]
  2793→    if additional_lookups:
  2794→        # Don't need to clone because the manager should have given us a fresh
  2795→        # instance, so we access an internal instead of using public interface
  2796→        # for performance reasons.
  2797→        rel_qs._prefetch_related_lookups = ()
  2798→
  2799→    all_related_objects = list(rel_qs)
  2800→
  2801→    rel_obj_cache = {}
  2802→    for rel_obj in all_related_objects:
  2803→        rel_attr_val = rel_obj_attr(rel_obj)
  2804→        rel_obj_cache.setdefault(rel_attr_val, []).append(rel_obj)
  2805→
  2806→    to_attr, as_attr = lookup.get_current_to_attr(level)
  2807→    # Make sure `to_attr` does not conflict with a field.
  2808→    if as_attr and instances:
  2809→        # We assume that objects retrieved are homogeneous (which is the
  2810→        # premise of prefetch_related), so what applies to first object applies
  2811→        # to all.
  2812→        model = instances[0].__class__
  2813→        try:
  2814→            model._meta.get_field(to_attr)
  2815→        except exceptions.FieldDoesNotExist:
  2816→            pass
  2817→        else:
  2818→            msg = "to_attr={} conflicts with a field on the {} model."
  2819→            raise ValueError(msg.format(to_attr, model.__name__))
  2820→
  2821→    # Whether or not we're prefetching the last part of the lookup.
  2822→    leaf = len(lookup.prefetch_through.split(LOOKUP_SEP)) - 1 == level
  2823→
  2824→    for obj in instances:
  2825→        instance_attr_val = instance_attr(obj)
  2826→        vals = rel_obj_cache.get(instance_attr_val, [])
  2827→
  2828→        if single:
  2829→            val = vals[0] if vals else None
  2830→            if as_attr:
  2831→                # A to_attr has been given for the prefetch.
  2832→                setattr(obj, to_attr, val)
  2833→            elif is_descriptor:
  2834→                # cache_name points to a field name in obj.
  2835→                # This field is a descriptor for a related object.
  2836→                setattr(obj, cache_name, val)
  2837→            else:
  2838→                # No to_attr has been given for this prefetch operation and the
  2839→                # cache_name does not point to a descriptor. Store the value of
  2840→                # the field in the object's field cache.
  2841→                obj._state.fields_cache[cache_name] = val
  2842→        else:
  2843→            if as_attr:
  2844→                setattr(obj, to_attr, vals)
  2845→            else:
  2846→                manager = getattr(obj, to_attr)
  2847→                if leaf and lookup.queryset is not None:
  2848→                    qs = manager._apply_rel_filters(lookup.queryset)
  2849→                else:
  2850→                    qs = manager.get_queryset()
  2851→                qs._result_cache = vals
  2852→                # We don't want the individual qs doing prefetch_related now,
  2853→                # since we have merged this into the current work.
  2854→                qs._prefetch_done = True
  2855→                obj._prefetched_objects_cache[cache_name] = qs
  2856→    return all_related_objects, additional_lookups
  2857→
  2858→
  2859→class RelatedPopulator:
  2860→    """
  2861→    RelatedPopulator is used for select_related() object instantiation.
  2862→
  2863→    The idea is that each select_related() model will be populated by a
  2864→    different RelatedPopulator instance. The RelatedPopulator instances get
  2865→    klass_info and select (computed in SQLCompiler) plus the used db as
  2866→    input for initialization. That data is used to compute which columns
  2867→    to use, how to instantiate the model, and how to populate the links
  2868→    between the objects.
  2869→
  2870→    The actual creation of the objects is done in populate() method. This
  2871→    method gets row and from_obj as input and populates the select_related()
  2872→    model instance.
  2873→    """
  2874→
  2875→    def __init__(self, klass_info, select, db, fetch_mode):
  2876→        self.db = db
  2877→        self.fetch_mode = fetch_mode
  2878→        # Pre-compute needed attributes. The attributes are:
  2879→        #  - model_cls: the possibly deferred model class to instantiate
  2880→        #  - either:
  2881→        #    - cols_start, cols_end: usually the columns in the row are
  2882→        #      in the same order model_cls.__init__ expects them, so we
  2883→        #      can instantiate by model_cls(*row[cols_start:cols_end])
  2884→        #    - reorder_for_init: When select_related descends to a child
  2885→        #      class, then we want to reuse the already selected parent
  2886→        #      data. However, in this case the parent data isn't necessarily
  2887→        #      in the same order that Model.__init__ expects it to be, so
  2888→        #      we have to reorder the parent data. The reorder_for_init
  2889→        #      attribute contains a function used to reorder the field data
  2890→        #      in the order __init__ expects it.
  2891→        #  - pk_idx: the index of the primary key field in the reordered
  2892→        #    model data. Used to check if a related object exists at all.
  2893→        #  - init_list: the field attnames fetched from the database. For
  2894→        #    deferred models this isn't the same as all attnames of the
  2895→        #    model's fields.
  2896→        #  - related_populators: a list of RelatedPopulator instances if
  2897→        #    select_related() descends to related models from this model.
  2898→        #  - local_setter, remote_setter: Methods to set cached values on
  2899→        #    the object being populated and on the remote object. Usually
  2900→        #    these are Field.set_cached_value() methods.
  2901→        select_fields = klass_info["select_fields"]
  2902→        from_parent = klass_info["from_parent"]
  2903→        if not from_parent:
  2904→            self.cols_start = select_fields[0]
  2905→            self.cols_end = select_fields[-1] + 1
  2906→            self.init_list = [
  2907→                f[0].target.attname for f in select[self.cols_start : self.cols_end]
  2908→            ]
  2909→            self.reorder_for_init = None
  2910→        else:
  2911→            attname_indexes = {
  2912→                select[idx][0].target.attname: idx for idx in select_fields
  2913→            }
  2914→            model_init_attnames = (
  2915→                f.attname for f in klass_info["model"]._meta.concrete_fields
  2916→            )
  2917→            self.init_list = [
  2918→                attname for attname in model_init_attnames if attname in attname_indexes
  2919→            ]
  2920→            self.reorder_for_init = operator.itemgetter(
  2921→                *[attname_indexes[attname] for attname in self.init_list]
  2922→            )
  2923→
  2924→        self.model_cls = klass_info["model"]
  2925→        # A primary key must have all of its constituents not-NULL as
  2926→        # NULL != NULL and thus NULL cannot be referenced through a foreign
  2927→        # relationship. Therefore checking for a single member of the primary
  2928→        # key is enough to determine if the referenced object exists or not.
  2929→        self.pk_idx = self.init_list.index(self.model_cls._meta.pk_fields[0].attname)
  2930→        self.related_populators = get_related_populators(
  2931→            klass_info, select, self.db, fetch_mode
  2932→        )
  2933→        self.local_setter = klass_info["local_setter"]
  2934→        self.remote_setter = klass_info["remote_setter"]
  2935→
  2936→    def populate(self, row, from_obj):
  2937→        if self.reorder_for_init:
  2938→            obj_data = self.reorder_for_init(row)
  2939→        else:
  2940→            obj_data = row[self.cols_start : self.cols_end]
  2941→        if obj_data[self.pk_idx] is None:
  2942→            obj = None
  2943→        else:
  2944→            obj = self.model_cls.from_db(
  2945→                self.db,
  2946→                self.init_list,
  2947→                obj_data,
  2948→                fetch_mode=self.fetch_mode,
  2949→            )
  2950→            for rel_iter in self.related_populators:
  2951→                rel_iter.populate(row, obj)
  2952→        self.local_setter(from_obj, obj)
  2953→        if obj is not None:
  2954→            self.remote_setter(obj, from_obj)
  2955→
  2956→
  2957→def get_related_populators(klass_info, select, db, fetch_mode):
  2958→    iterators = []
  2959→    related_klass_infos = klass_info.get("related_klass_infos", [])
  2960→    for rel_klass_info in related_klass_infos:
  2961→        rel_cls = RelatedPopulator(rel_klass_info, select, db, fetch_mode)
  2962→        iterators.append(rel_cls)
  2963→    return iterators
  2964→

     4→from django.conf import settings
     5→from django.core.exceptions import PermissionDenied
     6→from django.core.mail import mail_managers
     7→from django.http import HttpResponsePermanentRedirect
     8→from django.urls import is_valid_path
     9→from django.utils.deprecation import MiddlewareMixin
    10→from django.utils.http import escape_leading_slashes
    11→

    13→class CommonMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin):
    14→    """
    15→    "Common" middleware for taking care of some basic operations:
    16→
    17→        - Forbid access to User-Agents in settings.DISALLOWED_USER_AGENTS
    18→
    19→        - URL rewriting: Based on the APPEND_SLASH and PREPEND_WWW settings,
    20→          append missing slashes and/or prepends missing "www."s.
    21→
    22→            - If APPEND_SLASH is set and the initial URL doesn't end with a
    23→              slash, and it is not found in urlpatterns, form a new URL by
    24→              appending a slash at the end. If this new URL is found in
    25→              urlpatterns, return an HTTP redirect to this new URL; otherwise
    26→              process the initial URL as usual.
    27→
    28→          This behavior can be customized by subclassing CommonMiddleware and
    29→          overriding the response_redirect_class attribute.
    30→    """
    31→
    32→    response_redirect_class = HttpResponsePermanentRedirect
    33→
    34→    def process_request(self, request):
    35→        """
    36→        Check for denied User-Agents and rewrite the URL based on
    37→        settings.APPEND_SLASH and settings.PREPEND_WWW
    38→        """
    39→
    40→        # Check for denied User-Agents
    41→        user_agent = request.META.get("HTTP_USER_AGENT")
    42→        if user_agent is not None:
    43→            for user_agent_regex in settings.DISALLOWED_USER_AGENTS:
    44→                if user_agent_regex.search(user_agent):
    45→                    raise PermissionDenied("Forbidden user agent")
    46→
    47→        # Check for a redirect based on settings.PREPEND_WWW
    48→        host = request.get_host()
    49→
    50→        if settings.PREPEND_WWW and host and not host.startswith("www."):
    51→            # Check if we also need to append a slash so we can do it all
    52→            # with a single redirect. (This check may be somewhat expensive,
    53→            # so we only do it if we already know we're sending a redirect,
    54→            # or in process_response if we get a 404.)
    55→            if self.should_redirect_with_slash(request):
    56→                path = self.get_full_path_with_slash(request)
    57→            else:
    58→                path = request.get_full_path()
    59→
    60→            return self.response_redirect_class(f"{request.scheme}://www.{host}{path}")
    61→
    62→    def should_redirect_with_slash(self, request):
    63→        """
    64→        Return True if settings.APPEND_SLASH is True and appending a slash to
    65→        the request path turns an invalid path into a valid one.
    66→        """
    67→        if settings.APPEND_SLASH and not request.path_info.endswith("/"):
    68→            urlconf = getattr(request, "urlconf", None)
    69→            if not is_valid_path(request.path_info, urlconf):
    70→                match = is_valid_path("%s/" % request.path_info, urlconf)
    71→                if match:
    72→                    view = match.func
    73→                    return getattr(view, "should_append_slash", True)
    74→        return False
    75→
    76→    def get_full_path_with_slash(self, request):
    77→        """
    78→        Return the full path of the request with a trailing slash appended.
    79→
    80→        Raise a RuntimeError if settings.DEBUG is True and request.method is
    81→        DELETE, POST, PUT, or PATCH.
    82→        """
    83→        new_path = request.get_full_path(force_append_slash=True)
    84→        # Prevent construction of scheme relative urls.
    85→        new_path = escape_leading_slashes(new_path)
    86→        if settings.DEBUG and request.method in ("DELETE", "POST", "PUT", "PATCH"):
    87→            raise RuntimeError(
    88→                "You called this URL via %(method)s, but the URL doesn't end "
    89→                "in a slash and you have APPEND_SLASH set. Django can't "
    90→                "redirect to the slash URL while maintaining %(method)s data. "
    91→                "Change your form to point to %(url)s (note the trailing "
    92→                "slash), or set APPEND_SLASH=False in your Django settings."
    93→                % {
    94→                    "method": request.method,
    95→                    "url": request.get_host() + new_path,
    96→                }
    97→            )
    98→        return new_path
    99→
   100→    def process_response(self, request, response):
   101→        """
   102→        When the status code of the response is 404, it may redirect to a path
   103→        with an appended slash if should_redirect_with_slash() returns True.
   104→        """
   105→        # If the given URL is "Not Found", then check if we should redirect to
   106→        # a path with a slash appended.
   107→        if response.status_code == 404 and self.should_redirect_with_slash(request):
   108→            response = self.response_redirect_class(
   109→                self.get_full_path_with_slash(request)
   110→            )
   111→
   112→        # Add the Content-Length header to non-streaming responses if not
   113→        # already set.
   114→        if not response.streaming and not response.has_header("Content-Length"):
   115→            response.headers["Content-Length"] = str(len(response.content))
   116→
   117→        return response
   118→

   120→class BrokenLinkEmailsMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin):
   121→    def process_response(self, request, response):
   122→        """Send broken link emails for relevant 404 NOT FOUND responses."""
   123→        if response.status_code == 404 and not settings.DEBUG:
   124→            domain = request.get_host()
   125→            path = request.get_full_path()
   126→            referer = request.META.get("HTTP_REFERER", "")
   127→
   128→            if not self.is_ignorable_request(request, path, domain, referer):
   129→                ua = request.META.get("HTTP_USER_AGENT", "<none>")
   130→                ip = request.META.get("REMOTE_ADDR", "<none>")
   131→                mail_managers(
   132→                    "Broken %slink on %s"
   133→                    % (
   134→                        (
   135→                            "INTERNAL "
   136→                            if self.is_internal_request(domain, referer)
   137→                            else ""
   138→                        ),
   139→                        domain,
   140→                    ),
   141→                    "Referrer: %s\nRequested URL: %s\nUser agent: %s\n"
   142→                    "IP address: %s\n" % (referer, path, ua, ip),
   143→                    fail_silently=True,
   144→                )
   145→        return response
   146→
   147→    def is_internal_request(self, domain, referer):
   148→        """
   149→        Return True if the referring URL is the same domain as the current
   150→        request.
   151→        """
   152→        # Different subdomains are treated as different domains.
   153→        return bool(re.match("^https?://%s/" % re.escape(domain), referer))
   154→
   155→    def is_ignorable_request(self, request, uri, domain, referer):
   156→        """
   157→        Return True if the given request *shouldn't* notify the site managers
   158→        according to project settings or in situations outlined by the inline
   159→        comments.
   160→        """
   161→        # The referer is empty.
   162→        if not referer:
   163→            return True
   164→
   165→        # APPEND_SLASH is enabled and the referer is equal to the current URL
   166→        # without a trailing slash indicating an internal redirect.
   167→        if settings.APPEND_SLASH and uri.endswith("/") and referer == uri[:-1]:
   168→            return True
   169→
   170→        # A '?' in referer is identified as a search engine source.
   171→        if not self.is_internal_request(domain, referer) and "?" in referer:
   172→            return True
   173→
   174→        # The referer is equal to the current URL, ignoring the scheme (assumed
   175→        # to be a poorly implemented bot).
   176→        parsed_referer = urlsplit(referer)
   177→        if parsed_referer.netloc in ["", domain] and parsed_referer.path == uri:
   178→            return True
   179→
   180→        return any(pattern.search(uri) for pattern in settings.IGNORABLE_404_URLS)
   181→

   103→    def _get_permissions(self, user_obj, obj, from_name):

   110→            return set()

   112→        perm_cache_name = "_%s_perm_cache" % from_name
   113→        if not hasattr(user_obj, perm_cache_name):
   114→            if user_obj.is_superuser:
   115→                perms = Permission.objects.all()
   116→            else:
   117→                perms = getattr(self, "_get_%s_permissions" % from_name)(user_obj)

   120→                user_obj, perm_cache_name, {"%s.%s" % (ct, name) for ct, name in perms}

   178→    def has_module_perms(self, user_obj, app_label):
   179→        """
   180→        Return True if user_obj has any permissions in the given app_label.

## Django QuerySet, Query, Manager, and Filter Compilation Pipeline

Based on my thorough exploration of the Django fixtures in `~/workspace/go/agent-index-go/testdata/fixtures/python`, here's the complete picture of how Django's QuerySet evaluation and filtering pipeline works:
